Party_Details.Name: Chong Hon Kuan Ivan

Facts.Text: Prior to 1996-12-20, Chong Hon Kuan Ivan was one of the three Original Shareholders of Eureka Advertising Pte Ltd, later known as Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd, together with Chang Hong Kaye Jimmy and Neo Kee Choon Thomas.

With effect from 1997-01-01, Chong Hon Kuan Ivan entered into an employment agreement with Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd under which he was employed as managing director and chief executive officer for five years, with a provision for renewal for another five years from 2002-01-01 if specified conditions regarding gross negligence or wilful misconduct were not triggered.
From 1997-01-01, Chong Hon Kuan Ivan held the positions of managing director and chief executive officer of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd.

A board of directors meeting of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd was scheduled for 2002-02-08 to consider, among other items, the conduct of Ivan Chong as managing director and chief executive officer as referred to in a letter dated 2002-11-06 from Publicis Worldwide to Ivan Chong.

On 2002-02-09, an adjourned board of directors meeting of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd was held at which a resolution concerning the termination of Ivan Chong’s employment was proposed and put to a vote; Chong and Chang Hong Kaye Jimmy voted against the resolution and Neo Kee Choon Thomas abstained.
On 2002-02-09, a letter was sent to Ivan Chong on the letterhead of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd confirming that his employment with the company was treated as terminated.
By 2002-01-01, disputes had arisen between Ivan Chong and other stakeholders of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd concerning his employment and related agreements.
By 2002-01-01, Ivan Chong had commenced a derivative action under section 216A of the Companies Act in relation to matters involving Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd.

On 2002-02-08 or 2002-02-09, prior to the adjourned board meeting, Jean-Paul Morin informed Ivan Chong that Publicis Worldwide BV had a new settlement proposal and that if Chong did not accept this offer, a resolution concerning his dismissal would be put at the adjourned board meeting on 2002-02-09.

On 2002-02-08 or 2002-02-09, a new settlement proposal from Publicis Worldwide BV was communicated to Ivan Chong, which contained terms less favourable to him than those in an earlier settlement agreement between him and Publicis Worldwide BV and Publicis Groupe SA.
On 2002-02-08 or 2002-02-09, Ivan Chong did not accept the new settlement proposal communicated to him by Jean-Paul Morin.

On 2002-02-09, at the adjourned board meeting of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd, a resolution concerning the termination of Ivan Chong’s employment was passed by a 3-2 majority, with three votes in favour, two votes against by Ivan Chong and Chang Hong Kaye Jimmy, and an abstention by Neo Kee Choon Thomas.

Following the board resolution on 2002-02-09, Ivan Chong treated his employment with Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd as having been terminated and subsequently commenced an action against six defendants including the company and various individuals and related entities.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether individual directors and officers of a company can be personally in tort for conspiracy to induce and inducing the company to terminate an employment agreement when their conduct occurs in the course of corporate decision-making concerning that termination.
Party_Details.Rule: Under the common law principle derived from Said v Butt and related authorities, an officer or director of a company who, acting bona fide and within the scope of authority, participates in corporate decisions that result in the company not performing a contract is generally not personally in tort for inducing the company’s contractual non-performance or for conspiracy based solely on that participation.
Party_Details.Application: The events leading to the termination of Ivan Chong’s employment, including pre-meeting discussions among directors, the convening of board meetings on 2002-02-08 and 2002-02-09, the communication of a revised settlement proposal, and the subsequent board vote, were evaluated against the principle that corporate officers acting within their roles are ordinarily treated as part of the company rather than as external actors inducing the company’s contractual conduct.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge considered Levy’s application to strike out portions of the Statement of Claim that advanced a claim of conspiracy to induce and inducing Publicis Singapore to terminate Chong’s employment agreement. The court analysed the principle from Said v Butt, which provides that a servant or director acting bona fide within the scope of authority is not liable in tort for procuring the employer’s breach of contract, and reviewed subsequent authorities including De Jetley Marks, O’Brien v Dawson, Katz, Desimone, Kuhn, Welsh Development Agency, Idoport, Schmeichel, Einhorn, and Imperial Oil. The court rejected the suggested distinction that directors could be liable if they conspired before, but not at, a board meeting, finding that such a distinction was artificial and that company business is often conducted both inside and outside formal board meetings. The judge held that the relevant question is whether the directors acted outside the scope of their office and whether the bona fide qualification is displaced. The court found that the amended pleadings contained largely bare assertions that the directors acted outside the scope of office and that the allegation of a sole or predominant intention to injure, which is standard in conspiracy pleadings, was insufficient by itself to remove the protection of the Said v Butt principle. The court concluded that there was no sufficient additional allegation, such as fraud or other conduct clearly outside the scope of office, to sustain the claim against Levy. Exercising jurisdiction to determine the legal question at the striking-out stage, the judge refused leave to amend the Statement of Claim in the proposed manner and struck out the relevant parts of paragraphs 18 and 19 as against Levy, allowing Levy’s appeal with costs.

Party_Details.Name: Levy Maurice

Facts.Text: From 1997-01-15 to 2002-02-09, Maurice Levy was a director of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd.
At all material times, Maurice Levy held the positions of chairman and chief executive officer of Publicis Groupe SA, a public listed company incorporated in France.
On 2002-02-09, at the adjourned board meeting of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd, Maurice Levy’s vote was counted in favour of the resolution concerning the termination of Ivan Chong’s employment.

On 2004-05-14, Maurice Levy applied to strike out paragraphs 18 and 19 of the Statement of Claim in Suit 766/2002, relying on the principle that a company acts through its officers and that an individual acting within the scope of employment is treated as the company’s alter ego for purposes of a conspiracy claim.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Maurice Levy, as a director and senior officer of related corporate entities, could be personally in tort for conspiracy to induce and inducing Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd to terminate Ivan Chong’s employment agreement, in light of the principle that officers acting within the scope of authority are generally not treated as external actors inducing the company’s contractual conduct.
Party_Details.Rule: Under the common law principle associated with Said v Butt and subsequent cases, a director or officer who, acting bona fide and within the scope of authority, participates in decisions that result in the company not performing a contract is ordinarily not personally in tort for inducing that contractual non-performance or for conspiracy based solely on such participation.
Party_Details.Application: Maurice Levy’s role as chairman and chief executive officer of Publicis Groupe SA and as a director of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd, together with his participation in the board decision on 2002-02-09 concerning Ivan Chong’s employment, was assessed against the rule that corporate officers acting within their authorised functions are treated as part of the company rather than as separate actors inducing the company’s contractual conduct. The pleadings asserting that Levy acted outside the scope of office and with a predominant intention to injure were examined to determine whether they were sufficient to displace this general protection.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court accepted that Levy’s application could be determined at the striking-out stage and applied the Said v Butt principle, as developed in O’Brien v Dawson, Katz, Desimone, Kuhn, Welsh Development Agency, Idoport, and other authorities. The judge rejected the pre-board versus board-meeting distinction suggested in De Jetley Marks and adopted in Schmeichel as an exception, finding it artificial and unsupported by broader authority. The court held that the business of a company is conducted both inside and outside formal board meetings and that liability should not turn on the timing of directors’ discussions. The judge found that the amended pleadings against Levy contained bare assertions that he acted outside the scope of office and that the allegation of a sole or predominant intention to injure, which is standard in conspiracy pleadings, did not by itself remove the protection afforded by the Said v Butt principle. The court concluded that no sufficient additional facts, such as specific fraudulent conduct or clearly extra-office actions, were pleaded to justify maintaining the conspiracy and inducement claims against Levy. Accordingly, the court refused leave to amend the Statement of Claim in the proposed manner and struck out the relevant parts of paragraphs 18 and 19 as against Levy, thereby allowing Levy’s appeal with costs.

Party_Details.Name: Salomon Salto

Facts.Text: At all material times, Salomon Salto was Senior Vice-President of Publicis Worldwide BV, a company incorporated in the Netherlands.
At all material times, Salomon Salto was a director of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd.
On 2002-02-09, at the adjourned board meeting of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd, Salomon Salto’s vote was counted in favour of the resolution concerning the termination of Ivan Chong’s employment.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Salomon Salto, as a director of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd and Senior Vice-President of Publicis Worldwide BV, could be personally in tort for conspiracy to induce and inducing Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd to terminate Ivan Chong’s employment agreement, given the general rule that directors acting within the scope of authority are not treated as external actors inducing the company’s contractual conduct.
Party_Details.Rule: Under the common law principle associated with Said v Butt and related authorities, a director who, acting bona fide and within the scope of authority, participates in corporate decisions that result in the company not performing a contract is generally not personally in tort for inducing that contractual non-performance or for conspiracy based solely on such participation.
Party_Details.Application: Salomon Salto’s participation as a director in the board decision on 2002-02-09 concerning Ivan Chong’s employment was considered in light of the principle that directors acting within their authorised roles are treated as part of the company rather than as separate actors inducing the company’s contractual conduct. The pleadings referring to pre-meeting discussions and agreements among directors were examined to determine whether they sufficiently conduct by Salto outside the scope of his office to displace the general rule.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court’s reasoning on the liability of individual directors, including Salto, followed the analysis applied to Levy. The judge applied the Said v Butt principle and subsequent authorities, rejecting the notion that directors could be treated as conspirators merely because they discussed or agreed on a course of action before a formal board meeting. The court found that the amended pleadings contained general assertions that the individual directors acted outside the scope of office and with a predominant intention to injure, but did not provide sufficient specific facts to show that Salto’s conduct fell outside his role as director. The judge concluded that, on the pleadings, Salto’s involvement in the events leading to the termination of Chong’s employment was within the scope of his corporate functions and therefore did not support a sustainable claim of conspiracy to induce or inducing breach against him under the applicable principle.

Party_Details.Name: Jean-Paul Morin

Facts.Text: At all material times, Jean-Paul Morin was the chairman and chief executive officer of Publicis Worldwide BV.
At all material times, Jean-Paul Morin was the chief financial officer and/or corporate secretary of Publicis Groupe SA.
At all material times, Jean-Paul Morin was a director of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd.

On 2002-02-04, Jean-Paul Morin called for a meeting of the board of directors of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd to be held on 2002-02-08 to consider, among other items, the conduct of Ivan Chong as managing director and chief executive officer as set out in a letter dated 2002-11-06 from Publicis Worldwide to Ivan Chong.

On 2002-02-09, at the adjourned board meeting of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd, Jean-Paul Morin proposed a resolution concerning the termination of Ivan Chong’s employment and exercised his own vote and the votes of Maurice Levy and Salomon Salto in favour of the resolution.

On 2002-02-08 or 2002-02-09, prior to the adjourned board meeting, Jean-Paul Morin informed Ivan Chong that Publicis Worldwide BV had a new settlement proposal and that if Chong did not accept this offer, a resolution concerning his dismissal would be put at the adjourned board meeting on 2002-02-09.
On 2002-02-09, Jean-Paul Morin, using the name of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd, sent a letter to Ivan Chong confirming that his employment with the company was treated as terminated.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Jean-Paul Morin, as a director of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd and senior officer of related entities, could be personally in tort for conspiracy to induce and inducing Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd to terminate Ivan Chong’s employment agreement, in light of the principle that directors acting within the scope of authority are generally not treated as external actors inducing the company’s contractual conduct.
Party_Details.Rule: Under the common law principle associated with Said v Butt and subsequent authorities, a director who, acting bona fide and within the scope of authority, participates in corporate decisions and communications that result in the company not performing a contract is ordinarily not personally in tort for inducing that contractual non-performance or for conspiracy based solely on such participation.
Party_Details.Application: Jean-Paul Morin’s actions in convening the board meeting on 2002-02-04, proposing the resolution at the adjourned meeting on 2002-02-09, exercising votes in favour of the resolution, communicating a revised settlement proposal to Ivan Chong, and issuing the termination letter were examined to determine whether they were within his role as director and officer of the relevant entities. These actions were assessed against the principle that directors acting within their authorised functions are treated as part of the company rather than as separate actors inducing the company’s contractual conduct.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court treated Morin’s position similarly to that of the other individual directors. Applying the Said v Butt principle and the reasoning from O’Brien v Dawson, Katz, Desimone, Kuhn, Welsh Development Agency, Idoport, and related cases, the judge held that directors who act within the scope of their authority in corporate decision-making are not ordinarily liable in tort for inducing their company’s contractual non-performance. The court rejected the argument that pre-board discussions or agreements among directors could, by timing alone, create personal liability. The judge found that the amended pleadings, including allegations that Morin acted outside the scope of office and with a predominant intention to injure, did not contain sufficient specific facts to show that his conduct fell outside his corporate role. The court concluded that Morin’s convening of meetings, proposal of resolutions, communication of settlement terms, and issuance of the termination letter were, on the pleadings, actions taken in his capacity as director and officer, and therefore did not support a sustainable claim of conspiracy to induce or inducing breach against him under the applicable principle.

Party_Details.Name: Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd, previously known as Eureka Advertising Pte Ltd, is a company incorporated in Singapore.
On or about 1996-12-20, Publicis Groupe SA acquired 60% of the issued shares in Eureka Advertising Pte Ltd from the Original Shareholders, who retained 40%.

Following a corporate restructuring after 1996-12-20, the shares in Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd held by Publicis Groupe SA were transferred to Publicis Worldwide BV, a company incorporated in the Netherlands.

As part of the acquisition on or about 1996-12-20, Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd entered into employment agreements with each of the Original Shareholders, including an agreement with Ivan Chong for a five-year term commencing 1997-01-01 with a renewal provision for another five years from 2002-01-01 specified conditions.

On 2002-02-04, a notice was issued for a board of directors meeting of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd to be held on 2002-02-08 to consider, among other items, the conduct of Ivan Chong as managing director and chief executive officer as set out in a letter dated 2002-11-06 from Publicis Worldwide to Ivan Chong.

On 2002-02-08, a board of directors meeting of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd was held at which no substantive discussion took place regarding Ivan Chong’s conduct as managing director and chief executive officer.

On 2002-02-09, an adjourned board of directors meeting of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd was held at which a resolution concerning the termination of Ivan Chong’s employment was proposed and passed by a 3-2 majority, with three votes in favour, two votes against by Ivan Chong and Chang Hong Kaye Jimmy, and an abstention by Neo Kee Choon Thomas.
On 2002-02-09, a letter was issued in the name of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd to Ivan Chong confirming that his employment with the company was treated as terminated.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd’s termination of Ivan Chong’s employment agreement, following board meetings on 2002-02-08 and 2002-02-09 and related corporate actions, gave rise to personal tort liability for individual directors to have participated in the decision-making process.
Party_Details.Rule: In disputes involving tortious interference or conspiracy by corporate officers, courts distinguish between the company’s own contractual position and the personal liability of officers, generally holding that officers acting bona fide and within the scope of authority are not personally in tort for the company’s contractual non-performance.
Party_Details.Application: The board processes and corporate steps taken by Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd in early February 2002, including the convening of meetings, the passing of a resolution concerning Ivan Chong’s employment, and the issuance of a termination letter, formed the factual context for assessing whether individual directors could be treated as separate tortfeasors or whether their actions were attributable to the company alone under the applicable principle.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment focused on the personal liability of individual directors rather than on the company’s contractual liability. The court treated the company’s actions, including the board resolution and termination letter, as the corporate acts through which the alleged torts were said to have occurred. Applying the Said v Butt principle and related authorities, the judge concluded that, on the pleadings, the directors’ participation in these corporate acts did not, without more, create personal tort liability for conspiracy to induce or inducing breach. The decision did not finally determine the company’s own contractual liability but addressed the scope of claims maintainable against individual directors in the context of the company’s termination of the employment agreement.

Party_Details.Name: Publicis Worldwide B.V.

Facts.Text: Publicis Worldwide BV is a company incorporated in the Netherlands.

Following a corporate restructuring after 1996-12-20, Publicis Worldwide BV became the holder of the 60% shareholding in Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd that had previously been acquired by Publicis Groupe SA from the Original Shareholders.

On 2002-11-06, Publicis Worldwide BV issued a letter to Ivan Chong referring to his conduct as managing director and chief executive officer of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd, which was later listed as an item for consideration at the board meeting of 2002-02-08.

On 2002-02-08 or 2002-02-09, Publicis Worldwide BV put forward a new settlement proposal to Ivan Chong through Jean-Paul Morin, containing terms less favourable than those in an earlier settlement agreement involving Publicis Worldwide BV and Publicis Groupe SA.

Party_Details.Issue: How Publicis Worldwide BV’s shareholding in Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd and its communications and settlement proposals relating to Ivan Chong’s role and disputes formed part of the factual background to the conspiracy and inducement claims against individual directors.
Party_Details.Rule: In assessing potential tort liability of individuals for corporate decisions, courts consider the roles of related entities and their communications as part of the factual matrix, while maintaining the principle that corporate officers acting within the scope of authority are generally not personally in tort for the company’s contractual non-performance.
Party_Details.Application: Publicis Worldwide BV’s acquisition of shares in Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd, its letter of 2002-11-06 concerning Ivan Chong’s conduct, and its revised settlement proposal communicated in early February 2002 were treated as contextual facts the board’s consideration of Chong’s position and to the pleaded allegations of conspiracy and inducement against individual directors.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment referred to Publicis Worldwide BV primarily to describe the corporate structure, the transfer of shares from Publicis Groupe SA, and the existence of a letter and settlement proposal relating to Ivan Chong. The court did not make specific determinations on Publicis Worldwide BV’s liability in this decision, focusing instead on whether the pleadings disclosed a sustainable cause of action in tort against individual directors in light of the Said v Butt principle.

Party_Details.Name: Publicis Groupe SA

Facts.Text: Publicis Groupe SA is a public listed company incorporated in France.
On or about 1996-12-20, Publicis Groupe SA acquired 60% of the issued shares in Eureka Advertising Pte Ltd, later known as Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd, from the Original Shareholders, who retained 40%.
Following a corporate restructuring after 1996-12-20, the 60% shareholding in Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd held by Publicis Groupe SA was transferred to Publicis Worldwide BV.

As part of the acquisition on or about 1996-12-20, Publicis Groupe SA was involved in arrangements under which Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd entered into employment agreements with the Original Shareholders, including Ivan Chong.

Party_Details.Issue: How Publicis Groupe SA’s acquisition and subsequent transfer of a majority shareholding in Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd, and its involvement in the transaction under which employment agreements were put in place, formed part of the background to the dispute over the termination of Ivan Chong’s employment.
Party_Details.Rule: Corporate group structures and acquisition-related agreements can form part of the factual context for disputes involving tortious interference or conspiracy, but personal tort liability of individual officers is assessed under principles that generally protect officers acting bona fide and within the scope of authority from being treated as external actors inducing the company’s contractual conduct.
Party_Details.Application: Publicis Groupe SA’s role as acquirer of a majority stake in Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd and participant in the arrangements leading to Ivan Chong’s employment agreement was considered as part of the overall corporate and contractual framework within which the later board decisions and tortious conduct by individual directors occurred.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment mentioned Publicis Groupe SA to explain the ownership structure and the origin of the employment agreements with the Original Shareholders, including Ivan Chong. The court did not in this decision resolve claims against Publicis Groupe SA, concentrating instead on the legal sufficiency of the tort claims against individual directors under the Said v Butt principle.
